content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

July 6, 1983


Fred Lowell
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
225 Bush Street
San Francisco, CA 94120

Re: Advice No. A-83-155

Dear Mr. Lowell:

This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation
regarding Government Code Section 84308. You asked whether this
section requires contributions made by a parent company and its
subsidiary1/ to be aggregated for determining whether a
contribution of $250 or more has been made to a member,
alternate, or candidate of a board or commission.

In the case of a parent company and a wholly-owned
subsidiary, the contributions made by the companies generally
must be aggregated. A contrary result is reached only when it is
clear from the surrounding circumstances that the corporation
and its subsidiary acted completely independently of each
other. (See Kahn, No. 75-185, Nov. 3, 1976.)

In the case of a company having a subsidiary which is not
wholly-owned, there is no presumption that the two companies are
acting together when they give contributions. However, their
contributions must be aggregated if the facts demonstrate that
the companies are acting as one. Factors to be considered are
whether the parent exercises direction and control over the
subsidiary's contribution, or whether the contributions are made
pursuant to an implied or expressed agreement to act with a
union of purpose to accomplish a common political goal. (See
Kahn, No. 75-185, and Lumsdon, No. 75-205, Sept. 7, 1976.)

In both the Kahn and Lumsdon Opinions, aggregation is
considered in the context of the campaign reporting provisions

_____________________
1/ For purposes of our conversation, we assumed that the
subsidiary was not wholly-owned by the parent.

Fred Lowell
July 6, 1983
Page 2


of the Act, but their discussions are applicable to Government
Code Section 84308.

If I can be of any further help to you, please feel free to
call me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

(415) 983-1585

225 BUSH STREET

JUN 27

P.O. BOX 7880
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94120

TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

WASHINGTON, D.C.
1025 CONNECTICUT AVE., N.W.
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20036
TELEPHONE (202) 887-0300

SAN JOSE
333 WEST SANTA CLARA STREET
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95113
TELEPHONE (408) 947-4000

June 24, 1983

Ms. Janis S. McLean
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Janis:

Pursuant to our conversation yesterday, this
letter constitutes a request for written advice. As I
mentioned to you, this firm represents various corporate
clients, many of whom have subsidiaries. In some cases,
the political activities of a subsidiary are conducted
independently from the political activities of the parent.
Thus, for example, a subsidiary may make political con-
tributions without any direction or control from the
parent such that it need not file campaign statements
as an "affiliated entity" pursuant to 2 Cal.Admin.Code,
§ 18428 (see, e.g., In the Matter of Kahn (1976) 2 FPPC
Ops., 151). As we have discussed, in such a situation
a separate $5,000 major donor threshold is applicable to
the parent and to the subsidiary.

My question has to do with newly enacted Govern-
ment Code section 84308 which prohibits elected or ap-
pointed officers who serve on quasi-judicial boards or
commissions from accepting, soliciting or directing con-
tributions of $250 or more from any person who has an
application for a license or permit pending before such
bodies or from any person who actively supports or opposes
the application. It is my understanding that the Commission's position is that a separate $250 threshold is appli-
cable to a parent and its subsidiary in a situation where

the subsidiary, for campaign reporting purposes, is a non-
affiliated entity of the parent.  I would appreciate it if
you would confirm that my understanding is correct.

Very truly yours,

[Signature: Fred]

Frederick K. Lowell


cc:  Mr. J. Greenwood
     Ms. J. Pritchard
     Ms. L. Montgomery




2.

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-3662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6461         322-6444

June 28, 1983

Frederick K. Lowell
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
P.O. Box 7880
San Francisco, CA 94120

Re: A-83-155

Dear Mr. Lowell:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

[illegible]
Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

sections.question: You asked whether section requires contributions made by a parent company and subsidiary to be aggregated for determining whether a contribution of $250 or more has been made to a member, alternate, or candidate of a board or commission.
sections.conclusion: In the case of a parent company and a wholly-owned subsidiary, the contributions made by the companies generally must be aggregated. A contrary result is reached only when it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the corporation and its subsidiary acted completely independently of each other. In the case of a company having a subsidiary which is not wholly-owned, there is no presumption that the two companies are acting together when they give contributions. However, their contributions must be aggregated if the facts demonstrate that the companies are acting as one.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether contributions from a parent company and its subsidiary must be aggregated under Government Code Section 84308 when determining if the $250 contribution threshold has been met for quasi-judicial board members.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: For wholly-owned subsidiaries, contributions must be aggregated unless the parent and subsidiary acted completely independently. For non-wholly-owned subsidiaries, contributions are presumed independent but must be aggregated if facts show the entities are acting as one, considering factors such as parental direction/control and implied agreements to achieve common political goals.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated June 24, 1983) and FPPC response (dated July 6, 1983). Clear question and conclusion sections present. References prior FPPC opinions (Kahn and Lumsdon). Minor handwritten signature on response letter.

QUESTION: You asked whether section requires contributions made by a parent company and subsidiary to be aggregated for determining whether a contribution of $250 or more has been made to a member, alternate, or candidate of a board or commission.

CONCLUSION: In the case of a parent company and a wholly-owned subsidiary, the contributions made by the companies generally must be aggregated. A contrary result is reached only when it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the corporation and its subsidiary acted completely independently of each other. In the case of a company having a subsidiary which is not wholly-owned, there is no presumption that the two companies are acting together when they give contributions. However, their contributions must be aggregated if the facts demonstrate that the companies are acting as one.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel responds to Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro regarding whether parent company and subsidiary contributions must be aggregated under Government Code Section 84308's $250 contribution prohibition for quasi-judicial board members. The advice distinguishes between wholly-owned and non-wholly-owned subsidiaries, with different aggregation standards applying to each.
